_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Hawkins County, TN. Formed 1786 from Sullivan County. Seat: Rogersville. Records from 1786. The Rogersville Review (established 1840) provides newspaper documentation. Cherokee Lake (TVA, 1941) displaced some families whose property records may be in TVA archives.

previous_courthouses.fate: Early log and frame courthouses. Records maintained through early transitions.
Brick courthouse. Survived the Civil War. Replaced by the current WPA-era building in 1937.

record_losses.cause: None — no catastrophic fire or war destruction
record_losses.description: Hawkins County's courthouse survived the Civil War and has not experienced a significant fire. Records from 1786 are generally well-preserved. The county's early records include pre-statehood NC grants and Territorial period documents. Court minutes may show gaps during Civil War disruption but the record books are intact.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in Hawkins County after 1786, the records almost certainly survive. Gaps reflect the ancestor's circumstances (poverty, transience, mountain isolation) rather than courthouse losses.
record_losses.source: Tennessee State Library and Archives. Guide to County Records: Hawkins County.

deeds.gaps: No significant gaps. Pre-1796 deeds are NC grants.
deeds.repository: Hawkins County Register of Deeds, Rogersville; TSLA microfilm

wills_estates.gaps: Earliest loose estate papers may be fragmentary.

court_minutes.completeness: Good — continuous with Civil War disruption

substitute_sources.description: The Rogersville Review, established 1840, is one of the oldest continuously published newspapers in Tennessee. It published court proceedings, estate notices, land sales, marriage announcements, and obituaries.
substitute_sources.repository: Hawkins County Library; Tennessee State Library and Archives microfilm; some Newspapers.com digitization
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Excellent source for post-1840 documentation. Estate notices and sheriff's sale announcements name individuals and describe property. Obituaries provide family relationships.
substitute_sources.source: Tennessee Press Association. Tennessee Newspaper Archive finding aids.

substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families. Agricultural schedules (1850-1880) are particularly valuable for documenting farm operations.
substitute_sources.source: NARA. Federal Census Schedules, Hawkins County, TN.

substitute_sources.description: Cherokee Dam land acquisition displaced some Hawkins County families from Holston River bottomland. TVA records document every tract.
substitute_sources.repository: Tennessee Valley Authority Technical Library; National Archives Southeast Region
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Documents displaced families — names, tract locations, compensation, relocation patterns.
substitute_sources.source: Tennessee Valley Authority. Cherokee Reservoir: Report to the Congress. Knoxville: TVA, 1943.

narrative_hooks: Hawkins County's courthouse has survived since 1786 without a major fire — {ancestor_name}'s records are almost certainly intact.
The Rogersville Review has printed local news since 1840 — if {ancestor_name} appeared in an estate notice, a court proceeding, or an obituary, the microfilm holds it.
Pre-statehood records in Hawkins County are North Carolina grants — {ancestor_name}'s earliest deed may carry the authority of a state three hundred miles to the east.
Cherokee Lake displaced some Holston bottomland families — if {ancestor_name}'s property was condemned by TVA, the records are in Knoxville.
source_references: Tennessee State Library and Archives. Guide to County Records: Hawkins County.
Tennessee Valley Authority. Cherokee Reservoir: Report to the Congress. Knoxville: TVA, 1943.
Tennessee Press Association. Tennessee Newspaper Archive finding aids.
